Now it includes ordinary people in large-scale street protests” (Tang 2013a). Taken at face value, these campaigns appear to be examples of what is often termed “not-in-my-backyard” (NIMBY) activism, a pejorative term denoting a sometimes irrational and selﬁsh response to the siting of publicly beneﬁcial developments in one’s own neighborhood (Dear 1992). However, an examination of the aforementioned Panyu case reveals that a NIMBY discourse obscures the regulatory dynamism of environmental mobilization in China. The emergence of high proﬁle environmental protest in urban China poses important questions related to the “decentering”of risk regulation in an authoritarian country that has traditionally limited the role of non-state actors (Black 2002). Whilst regulatory regimes in Organisation for Economic Co-operation and Development (OECD) countries have become characterized by an increasingly diverse array of stakeholders (Black 2002; Hutter 2006; Gunningham 2009; Grabosky 2013), less is known about regulatory pluralization in authoritarian states. China’s leaders have attempted to facilitate a bigger regulatory role for non-state actors to tackle regulatory failure across multiple issues, including food safety, working conditions, and environmental protection. However, these top-down reforms have been described as “half-hearted” (Van Rooij 2012) because of the Chinese Communist Party’s (CCP) reluctance to cede control to independent third parties, which, in turn, continues to undermine regulatory compli- ance across a range of issues (Suttmeier 2008; Collins & Gottwald 2011). Nevertheless, opportunities for civil society actors to approach the regulatory state from the outside (Hochstetler 2012) have increased considerably since the beginning of China’s economic reforms (Mertha 2008; Van Rooij 2012; Van Rooij et al. 2013). For example, media commercialization has created market incentives for journalists to push the boundaries of state censorship and expose regulatory failings (Shirk 2011). Environmental NGOs have begun to pressurize Party-state ofﬁcials and corporations to comply with environmental regulations (Ho & Edmonds 2008). Digital technology can facilitate greater regulatory roles for Chinese citizens, including through“human ﬂesh search engines,” whereby netizens track down individuals suspected of regulatory (or other) infractions (Grabosky 2013). Furthermore, aggrieved citizens are increasingly responding to a range of regulatory infractions via contentious collective action. Indeed, the Chinese regulatory context appears particularly conducive to contention. As Van Rooij (2012) argues, because of the Chinese state’s tendency to award concessions in response to forceful protest, citizens affected by regulatory failure are more likely to obtain redress if they engage in contentious actions, thus, incentivizing contention and contributing to a situation of “regulation by escalation” (Van Rooij 2012; see also Cai 2010; Liebman 2011; Su & He 2010). Better understanding the conditions under which non-state actors play a regulatory role in authoritarian countries – and to what effect – is a vital component of a broader, cross-disciplinary approach to regulatory studies (Van Rooij 2012, p. 178). This article takes up this challenge through a detailed analysis of the Panyu anti-incinerator campaign. It draws on approximately 30 interviews with anti-incinerator campaigners, local academics, journalists, and NGO activists conducted in 2011 and 2012. I also consulted media reports, blogs, petitioning materials, and publications from Chinese academic journals. Although the Panyu case cannot be representative of all urban opposition to environ- mental risk in China, several contextual features make it well suited to an examination of the regulatory dynamism of this phenomenon. Guangzhou is one of the country’s most economically developed cities – in 2012 per capita gross domestic product exceeded US$17,000 (Guangdong Provincial Bureau of Statistics 2013). Panyu, meanwhile, is one of the city’s most mature residential districts. The area within an approximate eight-kilometer radius of the incinerator site, known as the South China Plate (Hua’nan Bankuai), houses 100,000 people, and includes several huge residential developments (Guo & Chen 2011a, p. 101). The biggest of these, Riverside Garden (Lijiang Huayuan), contains 12,000 households across 0.81 square kilometers (Li & Li 2013, p. 234). In addition, the Panyu case highlights the media’s potential for playing a regulatory role in urban disputes. Guangzhou is home to China’s most liberal and vibrant commercial media outlets, including the Southern Media Group, which has a reputation for bold investigative journalism. Many journalists live in Panyu, and the commercial press closely followed the incinerator controversy. Rather than presenting the Panyu case as another example of middle-class mobilization in China, this article is primarily concerned with how regulatory issues emerged and changed throughout the campaign. After the next section, which outlines the top-down process that determined the incinerator’s proposed location, this article examines the campaign in three stages. The ﬁrst stage, beginning in early September, focuses on the emergence of citizen opposition. During the campaign’s second stage, which lasted from late October until the eve of the 23 Environmental mobilization in urban China T. R. Johnson © 2014 Wiley Publishing Asia Pty Ltd 15 Page 3: November protest, the controversy attracted intensive coverage by the Guangzhou-based commercial media. The third and ﬁnal stage examines citizen protest and the wider legacy of this protracted campaign. This article then places the Panyu case in a broader context by comparing it with similar episodes. Anti-siting campaigns in China take place in a context of regulatory uncertainty, characterized by opaque and non-participatory decisionmaking processes, questionable government–business relations, and weak enforcement of planning legislation. This helps explain why regulatory concerns – including what should be regulated, who should be involved, and whether regulators should be trusted – are a central feature of campaigns against potential pollution in urban China. In the Panyu case, these concerns resulted in contestation between ofﬁcials who adopted a narrow deﬁnition of regulation as technocratic rulemaking supported by state monitoring and enforcement of incinerator emissions standards, and the public which, with support from the commercial media, rejected this state-centered approach. Instead, anti- incinerator campaigners advocated the incorporation of public opinion into siting decisions, and argued that upstream waste sorting and reduction initiatives were crucial elements of an expanded approach to regulating solid waste. Rather than merely focusing on end-of-pipe“command-and-control” regulation, this perspective highlighted the issue’s complexity and the need for more “decentered” regulatory solutions. Although government rhetoric changed after the protest to reﬂect a broader view of regulation that included a renewed focus on waste reduction and sorting, and public consultation, many campaigners found this unsatisfactory, and the problem’s root cause – regulatory uncertainty – was not addressed. Until that happens, public campaigns will remain a common, and logical, response to LULUs in urban China. 2. State-centered technocratic regulation Siting controversies in Western countries have long featured tension between decisionmaking by technocratic experts and the democratic rights of citizens to inﬂuence decisions that affect their lives (Borrás 2012). Proponents of the former perspective argue that neutral experts are best positioned to decide upon sites the public views as contentious. Rabe (1994, p. 45) summed up this approach as follows: Managerial or regulatory approaches . . . depoliticize procedures such as siting by substituting expert judgment for public deliberation or decision making by elected ofﬁcials. They presume that no entity can rival state or provincial agencies for understanding waste management needs and establishing preferred site location. But others have contested this approach, and, instead, advocate greater public participation in waste manage- ment decisions. Some have argued that the public can bring important local knowledge to siting debates (Fischer 2000). “Partisan probing” by citizens can counteract the biases inherent in supposedly “neutral” technical experts (McAvoy 1998). China’s approach to regulating incinerators is narrow and “state-centered” (Black 2002). Siting and monitoring decisions are nominally reliant upon technocratic expertise, rather than democratic discussion with stakeholders (Huang & Yang 2013). Although the 2002 Environmental Impact Assessment (EIA) Law states that local residents “should” be consulted about projects likely to have a sizeable impact, public consultation is minimal (Y. Zhao 2010). Public attempts to obtain incinerator emissions data – which include a ﬁve-year campaign by the environmental NGO Green Beagle – have been largely unsuccessful (Balkan 2012). The Panyu incinerator decisionmaking process conformed to this top-down technocratic model. In late 2004, the State Environmental Protection Administration (SEPA) and the Guangdong Provincial Government published the Pearl River Delta Environmental Protection Plan Outline (2004–2020) (Guo & Chen 2011a). This document speciﬁed that 33 waste treatment facilities would be built throughout the province, including two incinerators in Guangzhou – at Likeng and Panyu.2 The Likeng Incinerator began operating in 2005. The Panyu Incinerator site was approved in 2006, with the state-owned Guangri Group (Guangri Jituan) being awarded a 25-year build-operate-transfer (BOT) contract (Guo & Chen 2011b). The exact location of the Panyu incinerator – at Dashijie Huijiang Village – was only made public in February 2009, in a brief notice issued by the Guangzhou Municipal Government. It stated that the incinerator would begin operating the following year, and warned the public against obstructing the project (Guangzhou People’s Government 2009). But local residents did not become aware of the incinerator in signiﬁcant numbers for another seven months. The Guangzhou Government designates every 23rd day of the month “visitor receiving day” Environmental mobilization in urban China T. R. Johnson © 2014 Wiley Publishing Asia Pty Ltd 16 Page 4: (jiefangri), on which government departments allow citizens and journalists to question ofﬁcials. On 23 September, the Municipal Environmental Sanitation Bureau Director reafﬁrmed the project following an inquiry from a journalist living near the proposed incinerator site.3 The government insisted that the incinerator site was the most suitable one in Panyu. Apparently conﬁdent that the EIA would pass smoothly, ofﬁcials conﬁrmed that incinerator construction would begin shortly after the 1 October National Day celebrations. Local media reports alerted many residents to the project for the ﬁrst time. 3. Rights-based entry into the regulatory landscape Snow et al. (1998) argued that contentious mobilization is more likely when the “quotidian” of people’s everyday routines is disrupted. Suddenly imposed grievances, such as the construction of LULUs, are more likely to trigger public opposition than cases where health damage from toxic pollution gradually accumulates over several decades without disrupting daily routines (Auyero & Swistun 2008). After they learnt about the planned incinerator, Panyu residents quickly mobilized in opposition. 3.1. Community cohesion and regulatory distrust Opposition to the incinerator among local residents was virtually unanimous. A survey published in November 2009 by the Guangdong Provincial Situation Survey Centre (GPSSC) reported that over 97 percent of the survey’s 1,550 respondents “strongly opposed” the building of an incinerator at Dashijie Huijiang Village (GPSSC 2009). It indicated almost complete distrust in the state-centered regulatory system. When asked whether the authorities could be trusted to regulate the incinerator effectively, 92.5 percent of survey participants ticked the box marked “don’t trust, pollution in the surrounding area will be severe” (GPSSC 2009). Public opposition was closely linked to the paucity of the “exit” option. Although studies have shown how exit is often a solution in relation to disputes about labor regulation (e.g. by leaving an underpaid or risky job) (Lee 2007; Van Rooij 2012, p. 171) and concerns about food safety (e.g. by refusing to buy unsafe food) (Siegel 2013), it was not a viable option for most Panyu residents. According to the GPSSC survey, only 21.4 percent of respondents viewed moving away as a likely course of action (GPSSC 2009). Not only would this represent a major inconvenience, house prices in Panyu reportedly plummeted after news of the incinerator emerged. Indeed, over two-thirds of GPSSC survey respondents listed falling house prices as a factor in their opposition to the incinerator (GPSSC 2009). In a February 2010 open letter to the National People’s Congress (hereinafter referred to as the “NPC Open Letter”) (Anon. 2010), residents stated that, “under circumstances whereby [people’s] right to survival, right to health, and ﬁnancial rights are threatened, [societal opposition] is inevitable, sustained, and is the only choice” (emphasis added, on ﬁle with author). 3.2. Internet gestation and an expanded approach to regulation Following the 23 September press conference, public awareness of the incinerator “spread like wildﬁre.”4 The commercial New Express Daily (Xin Kuai Bao) reported the story under the headline “Panyu to Build Waste Incinerator: 300,000 Homeowners See Red.” Yet although journalists from the commercial press had broken the story, they did not cover the issue intensively for another month. In China, media censorship increases in periods around sensitive dates and events, one of which, the 1 October National Day, was rapidly approaching. One journalist was ordered to postpone further investigations by her bosses because of the sensitive timing.5 Further- more, with local opposition only beginning to emerge, the Panyu story was yet to fully capture media attention. Instead, the Internet was vital in promoting public awareness of – and opposition to – the incinerator. Technology-savvy residents debated the issue via the QQ online messenger service, and on community Internet discussion forums. These neighborhood websites – “a special kind of online forums [sic] bringing together people living in the same commodity housing estates, who have shared residential territory and property interests” (Li & Li 2013, p. 233) – provided a “safe space” (Gamson 1996) for the gestation of grievances and for strategic discussions between homeowners (Huang & Yip 2012). Although many neighborhood forum posters initially expressed strong NIMBY sentiment, this left them exposed to accusations of selﬁshness (Huang & Zeng 2011; Guo & Chen 2011a; Zeng 2012). Homeowners, therefore, reframed their opposition, and portrayed themselves as “rational” (lixing) citizens using fact based arguments © 2014 Wiley Publishing Asia Pty Ltd Environmental mobilization in urban China T. R. Johnson 17 Page 5: instead of “emotional” (qingxude) NIMBY actors motivated by selﬁsh and irrational concerns (Johnson 2013). This helped to depoliticize the issue and reduce rhetorical conﬂict with the local government (Huang & Zeng 2011).6 On 16 October, a dedicated section for discussing incineration was added to Riverside Garden’s online forum Jiang Wai Jiang (JWJ). Several residents with professional expertise in areas such as biochemistry, environmental science, and electricity generation, were particularly active posters who made informed contributions to online debates (Guo & Chen 2011a).7 Panyu residents critiqued the government’s regulatory competence from two dimensions. From a narrow, state-centered perspective, they claimed that local ofﬁcials were simply incapable of effectively regulating incinerator emissions. These critiques highlighted the uncertainty that citizens felt in relation to China’s regulatory system. As one campaigner put it, “in the EU, waste gas and other by-products of incineration are strictly monitored and treated, and their safety can therefore be guaranteed. In China, who can guarantee that these by-products are safe?”8 This concern was dramatized when approximately 20 Panyu residents visited the Likeng incinerator. After their visit, the group circulated online a list of alleged cancer victims who had lived near the site – 42 people had apparently died of cancer since the incinerator began operating, compared with just nine in the preceding 12 years (Johnson 2013, p. 364).9 A commonly expressed perception was that local ofﬁcials belonged to a chain of “special interests” that stood to beneﬁt ﬁnancially from waste incineration, and were loathe to properly regulate incinerators for fear of increasing operating costs.10 Second, residents advocated an expanded view of regulation that incorporated Guangzhou’s entire waste man- agement policy, instead of limiting the issue to the siting and monitoring of incinerators. They argued that the government’s approach to waste management was overly narrow, and claimed that burning waste without adequate prior sorting and reduction would result in the incineration of unsuitable items, seriously increasing the public health risk. This view incorporated knowledge from overseas countries, and counteracted ofﬁcial pronouncements that citizens’ opposition was unfounded because of the adoption of advanced foreign technology in the proposed incinerator. Regardless of their real motivations for framing the issue in regulatory language, the fact that residents used regulatory expansion to legitimate their claims was signiﬁcant, not least because it subsequently stimulated a broader debate about how Guangzhou should handle its solid waste. 3.3. Contention spreads ofﬂine As contention spread ofﬂine, campaigners drew upon prior experience of “rights-upholding” resistance, which Riverside Garden residents had earned a reputation for (Zheng 2008; Ma 2010). In 2002, homeowners had mobilized against the proposed construction of a 40-meter-wide road through their community (Cai 2005; Guo & Chen 2011a; Li & Li 2013).As Cai (2005, pp. 783–793) has documented, residents adopted a range of tactics during this campaign, some of which – including sustained appealing to authorities via appeals (shangfang), letters, visits (xinfang), and organizing protests – were also utilized against the incinerator. By late September, a group of concerned residents had already met to discuss how to respond to the threat (Ma 2010). In mid-October, local communities began to produce mass petitions, one of which attracted over 10,000 signatures. In addition, small groups of residents maintained pressure on the authorities through repeated petition- ing. However, many became disillusioned with the appeals process. As one multiple-petitioner stated: We engaged in shangfang, because at that time we didn’t know that shangfang is useless . . . we appealed countless times, but until now we still haven’t received a single response [to the documents we submitted] . . . it’s all a big con, [appealing via shangfang and xinfang] can’t help solve problems.11 Panyu campaigners also adopted innovative tactics. For example, in late October one resident suggested holding a car rally (shaiche dahui) to protest the incinerator. Within one hour, 121 people had signed up (Huang & Yip 2012, p. 212). However, police questioned the organizer on “suspicion of organizing and inciting an unlawful meeting” (Ma 2010). He was quickly released, but cancelled the rally because “the road is too narrow” (Ma 2010). On 8 November another resident rode the subway whilst wearing a gas mask and anti-incinerator t-shirt, and holding a banner reading “oppose incineration, protect green Guangzhou.” She was informally questioned by local police, a practice often euphemistically referred to as “being invited to drink tea” (yaoqing he cha). Apparently unfazed, she documented the day’s adventure in a humorous blog posting. Although harassment was a reminder of the potential © 2014 Wiley Publishing Asia Pty Ltd Environmental mobilization in urban China T. R. Johnson 18 Page 6: risks of engaging in activism in China, it also hardened the perception that ofﬁcials were behaving lawlessly, and may, therefore, have been counterproductive (Guo & Chen 2011a). Importantly, these innovative activities also contrib- uted to the newsworthiness of the issue. 4. The media’s role Guangzhou has the most competitive and vibrant media environment in China. Many customers prefer to purchase newspapers from the commercial press, often viewed as more credible than state-owned publications and a better reﬂector of public opinion (Qian and Bandurski 2011; Shirk 2011). Chinese journalists do not have free rein – unclear and constantly shifting boundaries between permissible and forbidden topics encourages journalists to self-censor (Stern & Hassid 2012). But the need to maintain readerships amid a highly competitive environment sometimes encourages journalists to push the boundaries of acceptability. As the anti-incinerator campaign gathered pace in the second half of October, media reporting increased dramatically (Mo 2010). Two Guangzhou-based commercial newspapers, the Southern Metropolis Daily (Nanfang Dushi Bao) and the New Express Daily, closely followed the Panyu story. Between 23 September and 23 December 2009, they published 88 and 57 related reports, respectively (Zeng 2012).12 These reports tended to be critical of the local government. For example, journalists frequently bemoaned the lack of public consultation in the siting decisionmaking process, and questioned the government’s ability to ensure that the facility would operate safely (Mo 2010). Because both publications came under control of the Guangdong Provincial Government, they were immune from direct censorship from the lower ranking Guangzhou Municipal and Panyu District Governments.13 Skeptical commercial press coverage persuaded local ofﬁcials to publicly defend the state-centered technocratic model, employing a strategy of “state attestation” (Suttmeier 2008, p. 142). Ofﬁcials’ views were reported in local state-owned publications, including the Guangzhou Daily (Guangzhou Ribao) and Panyu Daily (Panyu Ribao). Whereas the commercial press made frequent references to “public opinion” (minyi) and “opposition” (fandui), the state-owned media ampliﬁed the local government’s voice by advocating “pushing forward” (tuijin) the project “in accordance with the law” (yifa) (Mo 2010). Local ofﬁcials repeatedly emphasized the pressing need for the incin- erator to avoid Panyu becoming “surrounded by waste” (laji weicheng). They argued that efﬁciency should guide the decisionmaking process, and that residents should trust the government to produce the best solution to the problem, namely incineration (Zeng 2012). Ofﬁcials also reassured the public that incinerator technology and regulatory structures were reliable, and referred to the prevalence of incineration in “advanced” overseas countries to allay public fears. On 30 October, ofﬁcials convened a press conference in which they enlisted several “experts” to argue the case for incineration. As well as stressing that the incinerator would utilize advanced overseas technology, the experts assured the public that it would operate to stringent standards. In doing so, they projected an image of a predictable and reliable regulatory regime that was at odds with citizens’ perceptions. 4.1. Journalists as regulatory actors According to Zeng (2012), whose work draws heavily on in-depth interviews with key media ﬁgures, local commer- cial media journalists actively participated in framing the incinerator debate. By attempting to hold the government accountable whilst simultaneously refusing to endorse populist NIMBY sentiment, journalists “mediated” (Zeng 2012) between state and societal actors. As one Southern Metropolis journalist put it: At ﬁrst, the Southern Metropolis Daily took an objective and neutral stance. We witnessed the public’s irratio- nality and the government’s unscientiﬁc [behavior]. But as a responsible publication, we couldn’t simply observe this problem without preventing or exposing it. So, I think that the media taking a [more critical] stance is not necessarily a bad thing. Our stance was to help the government’s decision-making process become more open and more scientiﬁc.14 Although the commercial press was unsympathetic to “irrational” NIMBY sentiment, residents’ redeﬁnition of themselves as “rational” actors enabled journalists to focus on criticizing the government’s “unscientiﬁc” approach to incineration and waste management more broadly. The commercial media impacted the case in three main ways, through issue expansion, pluralizing the debate, and broadening the regulatory debate. © 2014 Wiley Publishing Asia Pty Ltd Environmental mobilization in urban China T. R. Johnson 19 Page 7: 4.1.1. Issue expansion Yang (2010) argued that events in China obtain intensive media attention under three conditions: through govern- ment promotion of such coverage, in response to a major destabilizing event that forces the government to open up to the media, and through sponsorship from “issue entrepreneurs.” The latter include media professionals, netizens, and NGO activists (Yang 2010). Panyu’s middle-class communities house a signiﬁcant number of well-educated, “hyper-empowered” societal elites, including government ofﬁcials, company bosses, lawyers, retired military per- sonnel, scientists, academics, and approximately 200 journalists (Guo & Chen 2011a).15 Acting as “issue entrepre- neurs,” and utilizing channels unavailable to average citizens, they ensured that news of the anti-incinerator campaign reached a wider audience. As campaigners themselves acknowledged in their NPC Open Letter: Because the [Panyu incinerator] project is to be situated in a densely populated urban area, it will affect many elites. They have plentiful societal resources (shehui ziyuan) that they will allocate [to the opposition campaign] to the maximum extent. It is therefore very easy for this [Panyu anti-incinerator] incident to expand and reach higher levels (kuoda he shengji).16 The commercial media, embedded in local residential communities, was instrumental in turning the Panyu case into a “big issue” (Zeng 2012). Many local journalists were networked into national media circles (Lang & Xu 2013), and the Panyu controversy became a national story, attracting critical reports from the People’s Daily (Renmin Ribao) and China Central Television – the major state-run national newspaper and broadcaster, respectively, as well as overseas media outlets. 4.1.2. Pluralizing the debate According to Huang and Zeng (2011), journalists and local residents engaged in “reciprocal activation,” working together to advance a powerful critique of government policy. The New Express Daily and Southern Metropolis Daily both established “reporting small groups” to follow the incinerator story. Journalists attended meetings with homeowners, and even provided strategic advice (Huang & Zeng 2011). They also conducted numerous interviews with campaigners and regularly scoured JWJ for information, thereby providing a platform for residents’ opinions to reach a wider audience.17 Journalists also introduced a wider range of expert voices into the regulatory debate. Some reports utilized interviews with“anti-incineration”experts from Guangzhou and Beijing.18 This counterbalanced the perspective put forward by government-endorsed experts, and also “certiﬁed” (McAdam et al. 2001) the anti-incinerator campaign. This was contrasted with the government’s 30 October press conference, which one journalist described as a “hot air meeting . . . [in which] a few government-endorsed experts were invited to brainwash the media” (Time Weekly 2009). Media claims that one participant in that press conference held numerous patents related to incinerator technology undermined the government’s position. 4.1.3. Broadening the regulatory debate Commercial media reports shared residents’ concerns about how the incinerator would be operated, and what role upstream initiatives should play in the regulation of solid waste. Journalists also widened the regulatory debate to incorporate the planning process. They questioned the EIA process, with one report quoting a Gua- ngzhou Chinese People’s Political Consultative Conference member as saying, “even though it’s not known if the EIA will pass, land has already been appropriated, and buildings have been demolished. This is a bit contrary to logic, what happens if the EIA doesn’t pass?” (New Express Daily 2009). This echoed the concerns of residents who had complained that the siting decision contravened laws and regulations (administrative review application, on ﬁle with author). Journalists also advocated public involvement in the planning process. For example, several media reports stated the importance of respecting public opinion (minyi) (Zeng 2012). Thus, they suggested a broader notion of regulation whereby the public (and the press) could and should hold ofﬁcials accountable for decisions. However, the government maintained that administrative efﬁciency should take priority over expanding dialogue with the public (Zeng 2012). This intransigence resulted in a large-scale protest on 23 November, which became a turning point in the campaign. © 2014 Wiley Publishing Asia Pty Ltd Environmental mobilization in urban China T. R. Johnson 20 Page 8: 5. Protest, government concessions, and the rise of policy advocacy 5.1. The 23 November protest On 22 November, the Municipal Government Deputy Secretary General Lü Zhiyi publicly reafﬁrmed the authorities’ “ﬁrm and unshakeable commitment”to waste incineration. However, this message, which failed to acknowledge any of the campaigners’ concerns, proved counterproductive. In the words of one resident, “Lü Zhiyi spoke with a very hard tone. Both hands were hard. This enraged us. So, that night we mobilized homeowners for the 23 November petition (shangfang) using QQ groups and neighborhood Internet forums.”19 The next morning, several hundred residents gathered at the Guangzhou Municipal City Administration Com- mittee (MCAC) (Chengguanwei). Petitioners held aloft banners opposing the incinerator, and some wore gas masks (K. Zhao 2011). After arriving, they were required to register before entering the building, with a maximum of ﬁve people being allowed to enter per registration form. Over 500 forms were ﬁlled out, but administrative staff were taking over 30 minutes to process each one (Ma 2010). Increasingly agitated petitioners then walked to the nearby Guangzhou Municipal Government headquarters, where they shouted slogans and demanded Lü Zhiyi’s resigna- tion. Lü eventually appeared, and stated that the project would not proceed if the EIA failed to pass, which reportedly helped defuse tension (K. Zhao 2011). At 2 p.m., protestors dispersed peacefully. 5.2. The public opinion compromise Despite growing public interrogation of the incinerator project, the protest became the event that shifted the government away from determinedly pushing ahead with the incinerator “according to law.” Later that afternoon, Guangzhou’s Vice-Mayor Su Zequn announced that the project would not proceed if the“vast majority”(daduoshu) of residents opposed it. Maintaining social stability now took priority over constructing the incinerator at Huijiang. From the public’s perspective, this announcement resolved the incinerator threat, at least temporarily. The authorities did not explicitly acknowledge that campaigners’ regulatory concerns had been justiﬁed. However, they promised to start considering citizens’ views. Therefore, a public opinion frame – which represented a middle ground between citizens and ofﬁcials – superseded the previous focus on regulatory failure (Huang & Zeng 2011). In the days following the protest, local media, whose reporting of the event had been“insipid”after journalists received a censorship order from the Propaganda Department,20 praised the government’s new attitude toward the public. For example, on 26 November, the Southern Metropolis Daily wrote: From “ﬁrm and unshakeable commitment to promoting waste incineration” to “if the vast majority of citizens oppose we won’t start construction,” the debate stimulated by the Panyu incinerator project is now developing towards a good, win-win, harmonious direction. (Yu 2009) Attention to regulatory failure was brieﬂy rekindled on 2 December after a netizen claimed that Lü Zhiyi had “close ties” with Guangri.21 This added to the perception that waste incineration in China was prone to regulatory capture. However, between 22 November and 1 December, 68 percent and 77 percent of Southern Metropolis Daily and New Express Daily reports, respectively, focused on public opinion, rather than on regulatory failure (Zeng 2012). This helped defuse tension, and provided the basis for a symbolically important meeting between home- owners and ofﬁcials. On 10 December, an active Riverside Gardens campaigner known by his Internet name Basuo Fengyun publicly invited Panyu District Party Secretary Tan Yinghua to meet homeowners. Tan accepted, and visited the complex on 20 December. During the meeting, which was keenly covered by the press, he conﬁrmed that the incinerator project had been “stopped” (tingzhi). Yet some residents remained skeptical. They subsequently noted that later the same day the Guangzhou Municipal Government had used the term “temporarily suspended” (zanting), indicating possible divergence of opinion. Lingering doubts about the project’s fate help explain the drafting of the NPC Open Letter in March 2010, which was designed to maintain pressure on local ofﬁcials.22 It was not until 2012 that the ﬁnal decision was taken to construct the incinerator at Dagang. 5.3. The campaign legacy – policy advocacy Following the government’s U-turn, some residents continued to advocate a more sustainable and holistic approach to waste management. For this small number of residents, the campaign parameters shifted from a predominantly “blocking” activity to an “enabling” one (Hochstetler & Keck 2007, pp. 19–20). Unlike reactive “blocking” mobili- © 2014 Wiley Publishing Asia Pty Ltd Environmental mobilization in urban China T. R. Johnson 21 Page 9: zation against perceived threats, participants engaged in enabling activities seeking policy inﬂuence over an extended time period, often in cooperation with ofﬁcial policymakers (Hochstetler & Keck 2007). However, enabling activities are much less visible, and are, therefore, less likely to obtain media coverage (Hochstetler & Keck 2007). Although commercial media supported and contributed to this “policy advocacy” framing (Zeng 2012), the intensity of press reports was lower than at the height of the anti-incinerator campaign. During this stage of the campaign, campaigners offered constructive solutions for regulating the city’s solid waste, particularly in relation to upstream processes. For example, a small number of residents began separating their waste, removing items such as batteries. They photographed this separated waste and asked the government how to deal with it. Through this action, they discovered that there was no adequate procedure for handling harmful waste.23 These efforts culminated in the formation and registration in 2012 of a new environmental NGO, Eco Canton, by Basuo Fengyun. Its main role is to continue the promotion of waste sorting. One of its ﬁrst tasks was to conduct a comprehensive investigation into waste sorting in Guangzhou in order to submit suggestions to the government about how to improve this process.24 The formation of Eco Canton highlights the possibility of convergence between environmental (E)NGOs and contentious citizens, and could signal an important new dynamic in the development of environmental activism in China (Peng & Wu 2013). But the Panyu case also highlighted signiﬁcant obstacles in the transition from blocking toward sustained, enabling activism that proactively addresses regulatory concerns. One such barrier was related to the considerable political and resource constraints faced by ENGOs in China (Ho & Edmonds 2008) – as of August 2012, Eco Canton had only four staff members working from a small, single room. Despite networking with more experienced Beijing ENGO activists, Eco Canton lacked waste management expertise. Another obstacle concerned the difﬁculty of sustaining “biographical availability” (McAdam 1986) among campaigners. As one prominent anti-incinerator campaigner stated: I’m not the same as Basuo [Fengyun], they [sic] are dedicated [to the waste issue], so they formed an NGO to focus on it. I have my job, I’m responsible for a company, I’ve also got a family and I have to work hard for their sakes.25 Another related problem was growing personal tension between some core anti-incinerator campaigners. For example, one campaigner was accused of “selling out” on an Internet forum after he expressed support for the incinerator. Another campaigner revealed that since the end of the anti-incinerator campaign she had constantly felt torn between different residents who she had befriended during the controversy, but who had since fallen out.26 Once the immediate threat of the incinerator had receded, it proved difﬁcult to sustain the regulatory dynamism of the campaign’s earlier stages. Perhaps the biggest obstacle to sustaining activism was that, after 23 November, the state-centered approach to regulating waste had been reasserted, albeit with a newly discovered rhetorical commitment to public consultation. The Panyu District Government had quickly announced a four-stage public discussion exercise concerning waste treatment. The ﬁrst stage, from December 2009 until June 2010, would involve a “big discussion” (da taolun) about the district’s waste policy. During the second stage, from January until June 2010 (partly overlapping with the ﬁrst stage), the government would set up waste sorting and waste reduction pilot projects. For the third stage, from January 2011 until December 2012, the government, experts, and the public would decide on the location of a“waste management project.” Finally, the fourth stage involved “scientiﬁc monitoring” of this project. Although it appeared that the government had opened up the decisionmaking process to the public, many residents who engaged with this exercise became disillusioned. Some bemoaned that their suggestions disappeared into a governmental “black hole” and were not adequately considered (Dayang Wang 2011). Others complained that ofﬁcials were pursuing a policy of “talking about waste reduction and sorting whilst pushing ahead with incineration.”27 They were also criticized for promoting waste incineration “at 2000 horsepower, whereas with regards to waste sorting and waste reduction it is like an oxcart” (Liu 2011). With the government back in control of the situation, and with the threat of contentious collective action having disappeared into the background, non-state actors struggled to inﬂuence policy through ofﬁcial channels. Despite the limitations associated with the transition from blocking to enabling activism, it should be noted that the Guangzhou Municipal Government has taken important steps toward a more comprehensive waste management approach that went beyond the relocating of an incinerator. In April 2011 Guangzhou became the ﬁrst Chinese city © 2014 Wiley Publishing Asia Pty Ltd Environmental mobilization in urban China T. R. Johnson 22 Page 10: to make waste sorting mandatory. It promulgated a regulation requiring households to separate wet and dry waste, with noncompliant citizens subject to a small ﬁne. In July 2012, Guangzhou adopted a “real name” waste sorting policy, with households being issued specially marked garbage bags to enable them to be tracked down if they failed to sort their waste properly (Wang 2012). According to a local professor who closely followed the incinerator controversy, one of the most important legacies of the Panyu campaign was to put waste policy – particularly waste sorting – much higher on the local political agenda, and to stimulate constructive societal and media debate about how Guangzhou should deal with its waste.28 This suggests that campaigns such as the Panyu example can have a more far-reaching impact than merely stopping the construction of an unpopular project, particularly when they stimulate legitimate and “rational” public debate. 6. Environmental campaigns in a context of regulatory uncertainty The Panyu case is not unique – popular pressure has led to the delay and/or cancellation of projects in several Chinese cities. Following the 2007 Xiamen anti-PX protests, which resulted in the project being moved elsewhere within the same province, other subsequent examples of urban mobilization that became national news stories have included: • Homeowners from Liulitun in Beijing’s Haidian District opposing the construction of an incinerator, beginning in 2007. The project was suspended and relocated (Johnson 2013). • Shanghai ofﬁcials suspending a planned extension of the city’s Magnetic Levitation (Maglev) train line in 2008 following protests by citizens concerned about possible radiation (Stern 2013). • Tens of thousands of Dalian residents marching against a PX plant. Ofﬁcials promised to relocate the plant to an industrial park (Watts 2011), but it reportedly resumed production secretly in 2012 (Watts 2012). • Several thousand residents protesting against the construction of a PX plant in Kunming in 2013. The local government promised to publish the project’s EIA report (Reuters 2013). • Residents of Jiangmen opposing construction of a nearby uranium enrichment in 2013. After extending the public comment period, the local government cancelled the project (Chan & He 2013). A common feature of anti-siting campaigns in China is that they take place in a context of regulatory uncer- tainty, which is characterized by opaque and non-participatory decisionmaking procedures, and procedural irregularities during the planning process – most notably in relation to the conducting of EIAs. As Ministry of Environmental Protection Vice-Minister Li Ganjie acknowledged in 2014, “the planning process in some areas and some departments may not be as scientiﬁc and rational as it should be,” and some projects “don’t share enough information with the public” (McDonald 2014). Regulatory uncertainty is intensiﬁed by the commonly held perception that local ofﬁcials are beholden to “special interests,” and place proﬁt above public health concerns (Tang 2013b). In China, public responses to an uncertain regulatory context are often manifested in calls for greater transpar- ency and public consultation, which have become a“master frame”(Snow & Benford 1992) for urban environmental protest in China. This provides a basis for ofﬁcials and citizens to meet midway and negotiate solutions to impasses – in most of the aforementioned examples, including the Panyu case, ofﬁcials responded to protest by suspending projects while promising to further solicit public opinion.29 Although this may be seen as a somewhat cynical attempt to appease citizens, the (sometimes tacit) acknowledgment that public demands for greater involvement in the decisionmaking process are justiﬁed arguably legitimizes citizens’ calls for a more open and consultative planning process. It also highlights deﬁciencies in planning procedures, which are an integral part of the wider regulatory process, and brings them to the attention of national decisionmakers. Concerns over public unrest stemming from planning process deﬁciencies have already resulted in some policy changes, including the stipulation in the recently updated Environmental Protection Law that ofﬁcials and developers must release a full EIA report to the public (McDonald 2014). Although this is unlikely to resolve conﬂict in the short term, it shows how citizen demands expressed as regulatory concerns can stimulate favorable policy change, even if they are predicated on NIMBY concerns. Beyond the commonly expressed demands for transparency and public consultation, there are also variations in how citizens engage with regulatory issues. For example, in localities where the commercial media sector is less well © 2014 Wiley Publishing Asia Pty Ltd Environmental mobilization in urban China T. R. Johnson 23 Page 11: developed, the state dominates the local media agenda, resulting in greater possibilities for polarized conﬂict and little or no scope for the type of critical, but constructive, regulatory conversations that characterized the Panyu case (Huang & Zeng 2011). Examples include four campaigns in 2012 against potential pollution in the cities of Shifang, Qidong, Chengdu, and Ningbo, which quickly escalated into violent clashes between citizens and the authorities (Peng & Wu 2013).At the same time, national newspapers – including those belonging to the Southern Media Group – often report and comment on these incidents after the event.
